data.title: Astera Labs: A Reset That Feels Deeper Than It Looks
data.description:
There’s something odd going on at Astera Labs (ALAB). The company is moving forward but the stock is behaving as if something is wrong. I have seen this movie before but I have never seen it happen so quickly. A 50% pullback without a corresponding fall in fundamentals means one of two things. The market got too far ahead of itself or it’s finally pricing a change that’s not yet visible. I’m not sure it’s the latter.

What struck my attention is the speed at which everything is changing. Just a few months ago, Astera was talked about as the glue that holds the infrastructure of AI together, almost like it is necessary for AI to exist. And it is. The problem is, it is being obscured by concerns that are perhaps more intuitive than fundamental. That does not mean they are not correct, it simply means they are not easily quantifiable.

The Selloff Is Not About The Fundamentals

What struck me about the quarter is not only the growth, but the number of moving pieces that are aligning. Revenue came in at $270.6 million up 17% sequentially and 92% year-over-year. That's not only strong growth, it's accelerating into the end of the year. That's not timing, that's momentum.

If I look beyond all the noise, what’s left is a business that’s still behaving as it should. The revenue growth more than doubled. The margins held up nicely around 76% and the product offerings continue to expand, not contract. The Scorpio story is real, Aries is still compounding, and Taurus is quietly expanding in the background. That’s not a business that’s losing relevance.

What’s changed is the manner in which these figures are being perceived. Take, for instance, the concept of margin compression. A 70bps movement driven by mix shift is being perceived as compression. A non-cash warrant impact on the balance sheet is being perceived as a concern. These are not imaginary concerns, however, they’re being perceived at a level that’s in excess of the underlying economics.

The other thing that’s happening is that the market’s adjusting to a different reality. Growth’s still high. However, it’s no longer unexpected. When you go from proving out demand to executing on that demand, you’re going to compress the stock even in the face of improving performance.

The Business Is Expanding Faster Than the Narrative

What I keep coming back to is the scale of what Astera’s actually building. It’s no longer a retimer story. That narrative’s just too old at this stage.

Scorpio’s becoming a significant contributor. However, what’s far more exciting is that it’s opening up another layer of the stack. Switching, scale-up connectivity, and ultimately UALink positioning are all causing the company to benefit from a far greater percentage of the AI infrastructure spend. When you hear the management talk about a $20 billion SAM opportunity in terms of the switching and a $25 billion opportunity in terms of connectivity, you might think that’s no longer aspirational. The problem’s that the market hasn’t fully adjusted to this new reality.

So, I think it’s interesting that this warrant agreement with Amazon is being thought of in terms of margin impact. This is a demand signal for $6.5 billion of potential spend and I don’t think that is just a validation but visibility into a market that most semiconductor companies will never see. It is a firm anchor into AWS’s roadmap, particularly as it relates to Trainium systems and custom AI infrastructure. Of course, it does mean that they have a dependency on Amazon that is hard to overlook.

When a single customer becomes that significant, the dynamic changes. Price power becomes less one-sided. Revenue concentration becomes more apparent and the path forward becomes partially dependent upon the path forward for that customer’s architecture plans. I don’t see this as a negative but I don’t see it as a clear positive. It is a trade-off between scale and control and right now, the market is focused on the lack of control.

ALAB’s Valuation Reset

The valuation of Astera Labs has certainly compressed and that’s the biggest change. It’s currently trading at 51 times forward Price to Earnings in 2026 and 36 times for 2027, compared to a much richer valuation when sentiment was still treating ALAB as a near-flawless AI infrastructure story.

In terms of sales it’s currently trading at 15.9 times forward revenue in 2026 and 11.6 times forward revenue in 2027. That’s certainly premium but no longer in the price for perfection territory. What’s most important, in my view, is that the fundamentals are still very good with EPS growth of 34% in 2026 and 43% in 2027, according to consensus estimates.

So, yes, the valuation has certainly come down but only relative to its own past excesses. I wouldn’t say it’s cheap but it’s believable. It’s no longer trading at peak-mania multiples but it’s still trading as if there’s a very clean execution path ahead in terms of switching, rack scale connectivity, and hyperscaler expansion.

Risks That Would Force Me to Reframe

The risk that’s most significant in this case isn’t competition, it’s losing momentum in the parts of the business that are going to have to carry that next phase of growth. If Scorpio X doesn’t accelerate in a meaningful way in the second half of 2026, that’s a risk that causes me to think twice. Not because that product’s not relevant; it’s because that’s a timing issue that’s more important than the result itself.

Another risk is ecosystem positioning. If UALink adoption does not pick up steam and becomes fragmented among alternative solutions such as Ethernet-based scale-up, Astera’s first-mover position will begin to erode before it is able to monetize its position and then there is the Amazon issue. If this relationship becomes a pricing reference point as opposed to a growth driver, margin pressures will be more difficult to counter through mix and scale.

While none of these factors rise to the level of current concern, they are the metrics I would look for in determining if this is still an execution story and not a structural issue.

Final Take

What GTC didn’t do was to create a new story for Astera Labs, it strengthened the one that’s already in place. What caught my eye was the way in which rack-scale architecture and high-speed interconnects are becoming central, not peripheral. This plays to Astera’s strengths nicely.

The focus on scale-up networking, custom silicon, and system-level optimization subtly reinforces Astera’s expansion into switching and connectivity layers beyond retimers. Nothing was said explicitly about the company, but it’s clear where things are headed.

At the same time, it didn’t eliminate risk, in fact, it did the opposite. It made it clear that this space is becoming increasingly competitive. However, I think it did reinforce that demand environment in a more robust way than a few months ago.

data.title: Asia Tech+: Ride The Tech Innovation Wave?
data.description:
By Emerald Yau, Head of Equity Index Product Management, Asia

Technology has always fascinated investors—and for good reason.

It’s a sector that thrives on innovation, reinvention, excitement and disruption. From dial-up internet via a thick cable to 5G connectivity at your fingertips, from floppy disks to cloud storage and from search engines to generative AI, the pace of technological evolution has been relentless.

For years, US technology giants have dominated both investor attention and global equity indices. But the US is no longer the sole gravitational force in the global technology race. Amidst today’s unstoppable digitalisation trend and the awakening of the need to become technologically self-reliant, a new tech epicentre is emerging - Asia.

Asia’s technology revolution: transforming at full speed

Asia’s technology landscape is undergoing a profound transformation. Breakthroughs in AI, advanced manufacturing and digital platforms are reshaping different industries across the region. The momentum is propelled by strong government support and strategic policies: tech self-reliance is now more important than ever amidst a cluster of economic and political disruptions.

Another crucial factor is Asia’s demographic advantage. Not only is Asia the home to half of the world’s population, but its internet penetration has risen to 70%, above the world’s average[1]. In fact, across China, Hong Kong, Taiwan, Singapore, South Korea and Japan, the penetration rate stands well over 90% on average. The region’s digitally native, mobile-first population is fuelling demand for online services such as e-commerce, gaming and digital health solutions.

And while Silicon Valley gains the headlines, Asia is no longer just a technology consumer—it is also an important technology creator. For example, China leads the world in 66 of 74 critical technologies, according to a recent study[2], most notably in AI and robotics. Korea is a leader in memory chips and 5G, Japan in gaming, and Taiwan in advanced semiconductors. Together, these markets are setting the pace for global innovation.

Key technology exposure in the FTSE Asia Tech+ 50 Capped Index (by listing location)

FTSE Asia Tech+ Index Series: capturing Asia’s innovation momentum

The scope of Asia’s technology businesses is vast—they span semiconductors, AI, gaming, digital health, advanced equipment and more. A single industry classification label cannot possibly capture all that innovation.

That’s why a multi-sector lens matters when looking at Asia’s technology investment opportunities. FTSE Asia Tech+ Index Series, launched in September 2025, is designed to do exactly that. It offers a multi-sector solution to capture the breadth and depth of the companies involved in Asia’s technology innovation.

Within the series, the flagship FTSE Asia Tech+ 50 Capped Index tracks the performance of the 50 largest pure tech and tech-related stocks listed across select Asian markets, including China, Hong Kong, Taiwan, Japan, Korea and Singapore.

The index, which is reviewed semi-annually, goes beyond traditional software and hardware tech firms to include companies driving innovation in areas such as e-commerce, the internet of things (“IoT”), digital health and advanced telecoms equipment. We apply a 15% company-level capping rule to mitigate single stock concentration risk. The rules-based, transparent methodology ensures investors gain exposure to Asia’s most influential technology leaders while maintaining diversification and liquidity.

Simply put, this index provides a transparent framework to observe Asia’s tech leaders and their role in global innovation.

Does Asia compete with or complement the US in tech?

With the rise of the “Mag 7”, US mega-tech stocks dominate many equity investors’ portfolios. As Asian tech is now rising in prominence too, investors may be concerned about being overexposed to tech companies engaged in similar lines of business.

For investors, Asia’s tech stocks could offer a compelling complement to US tech companies. While US tech is dominated by mega-cap innovators focused largely on enterprise solutions, Asia’s consumer-driven tech ecosystem encompasses a broader innovation spectrum and plays a critical role in global technology supply chains.

The recent dominance of large US tech firms has been driven by large contracts, subscriptions, enterprise lock‑ins and the related services: for example, Amazon’s (AMZN) AWS alone generated US$128bn in revenues in 2025. Meanwhile, AWS, Microsoft’s (MSFT) Azure and Google (GOOGL) Cloud together controlled around 63% of global cloud infrastructure revenue.

By contrast, Asia’s tech evolution has occurred largely mobile-first and across the region’s dense urban centres, with monetisation via daily consumer engagement, transactions and ecosystems: China’s “SuperApps” WeChat and Alipay show how a multi-function app within one consumer interface may support a whole range of services (such as messaging, mobile payments, e-commerce, mobility and entertainment) and the associated businesses.

Asia Pacific currently represents around 48% of global SuperApp revenue, led by China and Southeast Asia. Meanwhile, the US has fallen short on this trend, largely due to regulatory, cultural and legacy platform constraints.

Increasingly, Asia is also emerging as the manufacturing backbone for the development of global AI and cloud infrastructure: Taiwan and Korea dominate semiconductor fabrication and memory chip production, with Taiwan’s TSMC (TSM) alone producing around 60% of global chips and around 90% of leading-edge logic.

These differences between the nature of tech innovation in the US and Asia enable equity investors to diversify across innovation cycles and consumer ecosystems in their portfolios, essentially capturing the growth from enterprise-driven innovation in the West and consumer-led digital transformation in the East.

From a valuation standpoint, the Asian tech sector also trades at lower multiples than its US counterpart, potentially offering a more reasonable entry point for investors: the forward 12-month P/E ratios of the FTSE Asia Tech+ 50 Capped Index and Russell Top 200 Technology Index were 13.6x and 22.7x, respectively, as of end of February 2026.

Asia Tech+ trades at a valuation discount to US large-cap Tech

5-year history of forward P/E ratio

A lens on Asia’s digital future

Asia’s technological development is advancing at a remarkable speed. The region’s tech companies no longer play a supporting role in the global trend towards digitalisation; they’ve now advanced to become headline performers, complementing the US giants who have long dominated the stage.

For investors tracking this evolution, the FTSE Asia Tech+ Index Series offers a simple, transparent framework to observe and analyse the companies shaping Asia’s digital future. By covering multiple sectors and markets, it provides a clear view of how Asia’s tech leaders are influencing global innovation trends.

data.title: TeraWulf Brings Power To Hyperscalers - Outsized Rally Triggers Downgrade To Hold
data.description:
I previously covered TeraWulf Inc. (WULF) in November 2025, discussing why I had reiterated my Buy rating then as it finally neared my Buy Zones, thanks to its robust AI monetization opportunities arising from the multi-year contracted backlog and the growing HPC capacity.

This was especially since I was of the opinion that its bright long-term prospects far outweighed the near-term headwinds arising from the higher debt reliance and the volatile Bitcoin USD (BTC-USD) spot prices.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am downgrading the WULF stock to a Hold here, given their mixed return prospects from the outsized rally, the consequently expensive valuations, and the potential volatility from the elevated short interest ratio of 22.07% at the time of writing.

This is especially since much of their HPC capacity will only be energized from H2'26 onwards, with it implying their likely to have underwhelming top/bottom-line performance in the intermediate term, worsened by the capex/debt/interest expense risks on their balance sheet/adjusted EBITDA/adjusted EPS prospects.

WULF's Rally Doesn't Happen Accidentally

Since my last Buy rating, WULF has indeed outperformed with a rich stock price rally of +43% compared to the wider market at -0.5%, as the stock also outdoes many of its neocloud/hyperscaler peers in varying degrees.

It is apparent from these numbers that the bulls have not fallen for the cooling AI trade sentiments and the moderating Bitcoin USD (BTC-USD) prices by -44.2% from the 52-week highs.

Much of their tailwinds are likely attributed to WULF's excellent diversification beyond the cryptocurrency sector into the high-growth cloud computing opportunity, allowing them to weather the near-term crypto winter, with the much-needed backstop from Alphabet Inc. (GOOG)(GOOGL) also mitigating the market's pessimism surrounding the debt-ridden data center capex trends:

Rather than trading purely as high-beta proxies for token prices, the aforementioned stocks—originally valued almost entirely as BTC miners—are increasingly being viewed as artificial intelligence and high-performance computing businesses.

(Seeking Alpha).

These reasons may also be why WULF has been able to outperform the wider market and its neocloud/hyperscaler peers, significantly aided by their ability to acquire the much-needed power to energize their expanding data center footprint—one that is currently in short supply:

Everything we did in 2025 supports that strategy. First, we acquired 100% of Beowulf Electricity & Data, eliminating related party complexity and fully integrating power generation expertise into our platform...

This industry is moving towards integrated bring your own generation campuses. We are well ahead of that curve because we are fundamentally a power company that builds and operates digital infrastructure, not the other way around...

We understand grid behavior, and we know how to integrate generation, storage, and compute in a way that works for customers and regulators. There are very few teams that can do that credibly and at speed.

(Seeking Alpha)

The same has been observed in WULF's promising multi-year construction/capacity expansion guidance to 3 GW by 2030, with 522 MW of that capacity already contracted (at $12.8B in revenues) and another 1.85 GW in the pipeline, underscoring their extremely promising growth prospects beyond the 18 MW energized by the end of 2025.

Their growth prospects are further supported by the management securing:

  • a “long-duration site control at Cayuga, up to 400 megawatts at a retired coal facility with real grid infrastructure already in place, brownfield, power backed, scalable,” and
  • another site in Kentucky with “480 megawatts, a campus with immediate power availability, expansion potential, and strong state support,”

all of which support their excellent value proposition as a power/infrastructure host (interconnection rights/electrical and cooling infrastructure) as their customers provide the “gut,” i.e., the GPUs.

Readers need not fret about WULF's seemingly unleased capacity, as the management already hints at the extremely strong demand from “every major hyperscaler and several large AI compute platforms” and an upcoming 10Y/15Y “deal happening pretty soon.” I don't want to give you a drop-dead date, but we're in very, very active and substantive discussions.”

These developments lend credence to their intensified capex of $1.06B in FY2025 (+307% YoY), allowing them to rapidly deliver capacity to new customers at a time of multi-year cloud super cycle.

This is especially since WULF has already reported robust HPC lease revenues of $9.7M in FQ4'25 (+34.7% QoQ/NA YoY) and $16.89M in FY2025 (NA YoY), along with ~77% in adjusted profit margins in FY2025, near their “85% steady-state margin guidance as utilization stabilizes.”

With the management expecting to deliver 483 MW capacity in 2026, I am of the opinion that the company is likely to deliver excellent revenue diversification and HPC growth prospects ahead, albeit heavily weighted in H2'26 at ~69%.

Otherwise, given the ramping up of compute capacity, I am of the opinion that WULF is likely to report near-term bottom-line headwinds, attributed to the likely higher “tenant fit-out revenue and associated costs” and the “development and pre-revenue operating costs.”

These reasons are also why I urge readers to temper their intermediate-term adjusted EBITDA/EPS expectations, with any material bottom-line improvements likely to occur only from 2027 onwards.

WULF Is Downgraded To A Hold Here

For now, WULF appears to be well supported along its 100-day moving averages, with the ongoing sideways trading along the established support levels of $11s and the resistance levels of $16s already contributing to its reasonable RSIs and the moderating trading volumes while implying a further consolidation at current levels, pending any positive catalysts.

Otherwise, thanks to the +664% rally from the 52-week lows, it is unsurprising that WULF is expensive at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 21.63x, up notably from the 1-year low of 4.47x, the 1-year mean of 14.65x, and the sector median of 2.93x.

On the one hand, it is important to note that much of WULF's HPC capacity is only expected to come online in late 2026, with:

  • FY2026 is likely to bring forth a clearer picture of its FY2026 EV/Sales valuation of 22.10x, based on the current Enterprise Value of $8.6B and the consensus FY2026 revenue estimates of $389.13M,
  • If not, FY2027 EV/Sales valuation of 8.34x, based on the current Enterprise Value of $8.6B and the consensus FY2027 revenue estimates of $1.03B,

It is apparent that their ramping up of HPC capabilities remains relatively nascent, as observed in the HPC revenues only comprising 27% of its revenues in FQ4'25.

On the other hand, I am of the opinion that WULF's recent rally may have occurred overly fast and furious, as observed in the pulled-forward upside potential to my estimated FY2027 EV/Sales valuation of 8.34x to its cloud peers' valuations, including:

  • Hyperscaler/commerce/advertising - Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) at 2.58x,
  • Hyperscaler/advertising - Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL) at 6.75x,
  • Hyperscaler/enterprise SaaS - Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) at 7.89x,
  • Hyperscaler/enterprise SaaS - Oracle Corporation (ORCL) at 6.53x,
  • Bitcoin miner turned HPC provider - Core Scientific, Inc. (CORZ) at 5.99x,
  • Neocloud - CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV) at 2.98x, and
  • Neocloud/autonomous vehicles - Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS) at 3.28x,

With the outsized rally offering interested investors a minimal margin of safety.

At the same time, it is important to note WULF's still unprofitable adjusted EBITDA performance of -$51.1M in FQ4'25 (-382% QoQ/-2144% YoY) and the growing net debt of $2B (-154% QoQ/-785% YoY), with it apparent that the bitcoin miner/HPC provider is debt-laden as observed in their net debt to adjusted EBITDA ratio of -9.78x (given the negative adjusted EBITDA).

This positions WULF in a similar category as that of CRWV at 5.9x and ORCL at 3.13x, against the Software - Infrastructure averages of 0.64x, with it presenting their elevated exposure to debt risks at a time of intensified data center capex trends, which potentially triggers its near-term bottom line headwinds.

The same has been observed in the drastic increase in WULF's interest expenses to $80.24M in FY2025 (+305.4% YoY) and the consequently impacted EPS performance at -$1.66 (-690% YoY), worsened by the dilutive ATM offerings to shares outstanding of 397.6M in FQ4'25 (+13.1% YoY).

As a result of the mixed return prospects from the outsized rally and the consequently expensive valuations, the potential volatility from the elevated short interest ratio of 22.07% at the time of writing, along with the potentially underwhelming bottom-line performance over the next few years, I prefer to prudently downgrade the WULF stock to a Hold here.

data.title: Templeton Global Bond Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: Global growth data was largely mixed during the fourth quarter of 2025; third-quarter growth outcomes were strong in the United States, modest but stable in the euro area and negative in Japan, as the effects of earlier front-loading ahead of tariffs unwound. There was also some uncertainty over economic momentum in the United States as the government shutdown that lasted through October and continued into November affected data collection. Inflation outcomes remained mixed, with trends in developed markets somewhat higher, while emerging markets (EMs) saw sideways to lower moves. Global monetary policy largely seemed to be in the late stages of an easing cycle, with pauses and a slower rate-cutting pace becoming more common. The US Federal Reserve (Fed) delivered two 25 basis-point (bp) rate cuts over the quarter, bringing the fed funds target range to 3.50%–3.75%, though dissent over the moves among Fed members highlighted uncertainty over the outlook. Most developed market sovereign bond yields rose, while EM bonds showed mixed performance. The 10-year US Treasury note's yield rose two bps to 4.17% over the quarter. The US dollar (USD) edged higher on average during the period, even as most developed market currencies, with the notable exception of the Japanese yen, appreciated against it.
  • Overall Contributors: Interest-rate strategies contributed to absolute fund performance during the quarter, as did sovereign credit exposures.
  • Overall Detractors: Currency positions detracted from absolute fund results during the period.
  • Outlook: We expect global growth to be weaker than we had expected prior to tariff imposition, but we anticipate a recession is likely to be avoided.

Performance Review

  • Interest-Rate Strategies: Developed market sovereign bond yields generally rose over the quarter, while EM sovereign bond yields were mixed. Duration exposures in South Africa and Brazil contributed to absolute fund performance, while duration exposure in Colombia detracted. We hold positions in select countries where we have identified opportunities from inflation and interest-rate outlooks, or where we see positive fundamentals in a range of factors, including fiscal progress, other economic reforms and reshoring.
  • Credit: EM debt indexes largely rose during the period. USD-denominated sovereign credits contributed to absolute fund return.
  • Currencies: The USD strengthened early in the quarter, but weakened in November and December. Positions in the Japanese yen and Brazilian real detracted from absolute results, as did the fund's net-negative exposure to the Chinese yuan. However, positions in the South African rand, Ghanaian cedi, Malaysian ringgit and Colombian peso contributed to absolute fund performance. We believe the USD remains overvalued against a number of currencies.
  • Positioning: We continue to hold select exposures in both developed markets and EMs. Our currency positions focus on potential upside based on our assessment of undervaluation against the USD. These countries tend to have robust balances of payments and growth fundamentals. In bonds, we hold positions in countries where we identify supportive fundamentals. We aim for a relatively high overall portfolio yield by holding higher-yielding local-currency positions in specific EMs that we view as having attractive risk-adjusted yields. Taking all of these factors into account, our top local-currency exposures include Japan, Brazil, Mexico, Australia, India, Malaysia and South Africa. We hold a net-negative position against the Chinese yuan, which has been providing positive carry. We expect this position to also help hedge our portfolios against potential risks to other EM currencies. In hard-currency-denominated sovereign credit positions, we favor EMs with improving fundamentals and policy responses. In all positions, we closely monitor idiosyncratic factors in different countries to identify relative valuation opportunities. We remain highly selective at the sovereign level, given significant variations in economic fundamentals and policy responses.

Outlook

  • We expect global growth to be weaker than we had expected prior to tariff imposition, but we anticipate a recession is likely to be avoided. We are cognizant that some of the higher growth recorded in some countries earlier in 2025 was likely front-loading ahead of tariffs, and we are watchful for further signs of possible payback through lower growth in coming quarters.
  • Inflation remains of some concern for now, particularly in developed markets. We continue to monitor outcomes to discern whether, and where, tariff increases might become more permanently embedded in inflation, especially as surveys across a range of countries still show higher inflation expectations compared to before tariff announcements.
  • We maintain the view that the USD is broadly overvalued, and we expect further depreciation over the medium term for both cyclical and structural reasons, though we note that the magnitude of further weakness may not be as great as was seen during 2025.
  • The fiscal outlook in a number of countries remains concerning. Already-high debt levels in major economies are set to rise further, while some political developments also complicate efforts to consolidate fiscal expenditures. In our view, such fiscal burdens raise risks for longer-dated maturities in the bond market.

data.title: Microsoft Stock Makes No Sense, Play The Spending Versus Revenue Gap
data.description:
Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) reported its second-quarter fiscal 2026 earnings in January, and based on the numbers, it was a solid beat. Revenue came in at $81.3 billion, topping both the Wall Street consensus by 1.24% and management's own guidance range of $79.5 billion to $80.6 billion.

Meanwhile, diluted EPS (non-GAAP) hit $4.14, topping estimates by 5.69%. Not only that, Azure is all but sold out. The fact that the stock dropped 19% as a result makes no sense. On paper, this was a beat-and-raise quarter, the kind of report that generally gets rewarded.

However, the market did what it did and focused on how fast Microsoft is spending compared with how fast that money is turning into revenue. Classic overreaction given the company's pipeline, which this analysis will cover.

With that in mind, my rating for Microsoft remains a Strong Buy amid the selloff.

What was in Microsoft’s second-quarter fiscal 2026 earnings report?

Under the hood, one of the most notable aspects of Microsoft’s latest earnings report is its cloud business.

For the first time, Microsoft Cloud revenue crossed the $50 billion mark in a single quarter, growing 26% year over year to $51.5 billion. That is a notable milestone. Microsoft is now generating over $200 billion in annualized revenue from cloud services alone.

Within the cloud, Azure and other cloud services increased 39%. That’s a notable growth rate for a business this size, though it’s a step down from the 40% growth rate it posted in the prior first quarter, which likely contributed to Wall Street’s negative reaction despite the overall quarterly beat.

Meanwhile, the broader Intelligent Cloud business brought in $32.9 billion, which is up 29% from the same period last year. This segment includes Azure, along with server products and enterprise services.

Then there’s Productivity and Business Processes, housing Office 365, LinkedIn, and Dynamics 365. This segment’s revenue for the recent quarter increased 16% to $34.1 billion. This business remains Microsoft’s workhorse, as it doesn’t grab the headlines that cloud and AI businesses do, but it still pulls in a good amount of cash with strong margins.

Now, one of the areas closely worth watching is the Copilot rollout. This is Microsoft’s AI assistant, which is being integrated into Office 365 apps like Word and Teams. Early signs suggest it’s helping with enterprise renewal rates, so companies are potentially renewing or upgrading their Microsoft subscriptions, but it is still too early to assume that trend is largely driven by the Copilot rollout.

And then there’s the More Personal Computing segment, which appears to be the current weak point of Microsoft. This segment covers Windows, Xbox, Surface, and search, and the quarter's revenue declined 3% to $14.3 billion, with the Xbox content and services segment falling 5% year over year. This segment has been underperforming for some time.

That said, I think most investors don’t look at this as a dealbreaker, as it’s not where Microsoft is directing the growth dollars. It’s a drag, for sure, but it is not especially concerning.

In terms of profitability, net income for the quarter jumped 60% to $38.5 billion, or $5.18 per share (basic), and while that is impressive, there’s a catch. Microsoft booked $7.6 billion in gains from OpenAI (OPENAI)-related investments during this quarter, while in the same quarter last year, Microsoft actually recorded $939 million in losses from OpenAI.

So, the year-over-year swing from OpenAI items alone was roughly $8.5 billion. When you strip that out, non-GAAP net income growth is 23%, which is the number from the actual operations, and it’s one of the metrics most worth watching. Still, reporting 23% growth for a company bringing in over $305 billion in annual revenue is strong by any measure.

It also tells us that revenue growth is translating into earnings growth at a healthy rate. But there’s also a point of contention to be aware of. Gross margin for the quarter narrowed to 68% from around 69% in the same period last year, driven by the shift toward AI training and inference workloads in the cloud. In other words, running AI is more expensive than running Office 365, and that is showing up in the margins.

That said, this margin compression doesn’t appear permanent, though it could mean another four to six quarters before margins finally level out. It won’t be a quick fix, so investors should size their expectations accordingly.

Why the stock dropped ~19% since earnings

At the time of publication, Microsoft shares trade just under $390 a share, and since the latest quarterly report, the stock has been down roughly 19%. Year to date, it’s also down around 20%. Over the past six months, the stock is down about 25%. And if you zoom out to a 52-week period, Microsoft is flat. For a company of this caliber, that kind of price action is highly unusual.

But what happened? Again, the market focused on spending growing faster than Azure revenue. Capital expenditures jumped over 66% to $37.5 billion year over year, while Azure grew 39% over the same period. So, the gap between spending growth and revenue growth made the market uncomfortable.

At the same time, Microsoft decided to route new GPU capacity toward internal products like Copilot rather than allocating all of it to external Azure customers. That appears to be a strategic decision that makes sense over a three- to five-year window, but it concerned investors who wanted to see spending show up as immediate revenue acceleration.

While it's understandable, the reaction appears overdone, but it still created an opportunity for long-term investors to buy more.

Valuation

At around $390 per share, Microsoft trades at a forward P/E (GAAP) of about 22.61x. This ratio shows how much investors are paying for every dollar of earnings the company is expected to generate over the next 12 months. A lower number generally means you’re paying less for future profit.

In this case, Microsoft’s ~22x multiple is about 21% below the sector median. The largest enterprise company on the planet, the second-largest cloud provider, and arguably the company with the deepest AI integration strategy in the industry is trading at a discount to the average tech stock—that is remarkable.

Here’s a table that lines up Microsoft’s P/E ratio against other Magnificent 7 logos:

As you can see, Microsoft and Nvidia are the only two MAG7 names trading below the sector median P/E. Nvidia sits at 23.71% below the sector median, which makes it potentially undervalued as well. Then look at the rest; everyone trades above the sector median.

So, the idea that Microsoft is expensive doesn’t really hold water, especially when you compare it against the peer group. The market appears to have repriced Microsoft to reflect the spending and other subtle concerns all in one move, but that repricing appears to have overshot. When you strip away the near-term noise around spending and margins, the core business is performing at a very high level. And the current multiple doesn’t reflect that. That gap between price and fundamentals is where the opportunity lives.

What could propel Microsoft stock even higher?

So if the valuation looks attractive, what does Microsoft have in the pipeline that could push the stock higher from here?

The key factor I see is the $625 billion in commercial remaining performance obligations, or RPO. It’s practically the company’s order book, meaning revenue that customers have already committed to, but that has not yet been delivered. It’s essentially the “almost sure” backlog. Microsoft’s RPO increased 110% year over year to $625 billion, and the portion expected to be recognized within the next 12 months increased 39%.

But Microsoft has been upfront regarding the RPO. They confirmed that 45% of that backlog is tied to the expanded partnership with OpenAI, which is a huge chunk. More importantly, the remaining 55% of the pie increased 28% all on its own, which is what’s more important. It means that even if the OpenAI component were completely set aside, Microsoft’s contracted pipeline would still be growing at an impressive pace.

Now, CFO Amy Hood disclosed during the earnings call that the full $625 billion backlog has a weighted average duration of about 2.5 years. Against Microsoft’s current annual revenue base of roughly $305 billion, that works out to around $250 billion per year in revenue recognized from backlog alone. While that doesn’t exceed current revenue, it does cover a significant portion of it and reinforces the strength and visibility of Microsoft’s forward demand.

Moreover, there’s another layer that the market seems to be overlooking. Hood also disclosed that Microsoft holds an $80 billion unfulfilled Azure order backlog due to power constraints. That’s $80 billion in pent-up demand just sitting there, waiting for infrastructure to catch up.

That also ties directly to Azure’s sequential deceleration. According to Hood, Azure growth would have exceeded 40% if Microsoft had routed all the new GPU capacity from the first to the second quarter exclusively to external Azure customers. Instead, Microsoft decided to invest in building out the first-party AI advantages at the cost of making Azure's growth numbers look slower in the near term.

That also appears to make sense over the long run, as it builds a product moat that compounds over time. But it did cost the stock on earnings day, because on the surface, it made Azure growth look like it was cooling down, when in reality, the demand is stronger than ever. It’s the capacity that needs to catch up.

What could continue weighing on Microsoft stock?

Of course, we can’t skip over the risks. Right now, and in the foreseeable future, the CapEx-to-return gap could continue to weigh, no matter how management justifies it. In the recent quarter, Microsoft's spending increased 66% to $37.5 billion compared to the same period last year, and the first half of FY2026 alone totaled $72.4 billion.

Now, the issue isn’t that Microsoft is spending massively. In fact, every major cloud provider is doing the same thing, with Amazon being the highest, with projected 2026 spending of $200 billion. The issue, rather, is the growing gap between how fast that spending is ramping up and how fast the revenue it's meant to generate arrives. Azure, on the other hand, is growing at 39% year over year and decelerating.

On top of that, two-thirds of Microsoft’s spending went to GPUs and CPUs that carry a six-year depreciation schedule (useful life). This is important because once you buy those chips, the depreciation expenses are locked in. Cloud gross margin has already come down from 72% to roughly 65% in about five quarters, and management guided for further declines in the third quarter. Meanwhile, the third-quarter growth guidance came in at 37% to 38%, which means continued deceleration.

What’s my verdict?

Microsoft appears to be facing a timing issue. Revenue is coming in, but it isn’t showing up on the same quarterly cadence as spending.

That’s because building out cloud infrastructure is inherently lumpy. There’s a natural gap between when the money goes out and when the money comes back, and it seems like the market is pricing the spending today but discounting the revenue that follows, and that creates an attractive setup for those with a long-term horizon.

When infrastructure catches up to the contracted demand, and it will because the contracts are already signed, the operating leverage on the other side will be meaningful. With that, my Strong Buy rating for Microsoft remains firmly in place.

data.title: Goldman Sachs: Capital Markets Titan At A Discounted Valuation
data.description:
Goldman Sachs (GS) has been a prime beneficiary of elevated market levels in the past year through surging equity trading revenues and investment banking deals. The stock is down 7.4% YTD versus the banking sector's 4.5% decline, despite outperforming in a one-year period. We believe Goldman Sachs remains a leader in investment banking, and the markets might have cast too much of a worry over its benign exposure to software private credit.

Have global markets peaked?

Investors will have no doubt that the stock trades at a strong correlation with equity and fixed income markets, as its Global Banking & Markets division and Asset & Wealth Management are responsible for 80% and 19% of its total pre-tax income, respectively, in FY2025. With the S&P 500 down ~3% on the year and valuations at a peak, investors have fair concerns about whether GS has topped as well.

While we are wary of the risks rich market premiums may pose, we opine that GS has structural advantages that can still help it thrive in a moderate market downturn.

Investments in equity trading are paying off

According to the Q4 call, the management has attributed its record setting equities net revenues during the quarter to multi-year investments in its prime brokerage and equity financing franchise that have paid off. The shift in institutional behavior towards more active repositioning, driven by AI-related equity rotations and geopolitical uncertainty, has facilitated higher trading volume. Further, the company has also benefited from structural expansion of derivative activities across rates, credit, and equity volatility. We believe that GS benefits more so than its peers when capturing this structural elevation in market activity—in the table, GS has notably higher pre-tax income margins for capital markets/trading segments.

Segment (FY2025) GS MS equiv. Citi equiv. DB equiv.
Capital Markets / IB GBM 42.4% IS 34.0% Markets 27.0% IB ~35%
Wealth / Asset Mgmt AWM 24.7% WM 29.3% Wealth 17.4% Private Bank ~24%​
Asset Management (included in AWM) IM 22.7% Services 33.6% DWS 29.1%​

Source: Companies, Himalayas Research estimates

M&A dominance and banking backlog

GS has reclaimed the top spot in global M&A advisory in 2025, having advised on $1.48T in deal volume and earned $4.6B in fees. Those deals included Alphabet's (GOOGL)(GOOG) $32B acquisition of cloud firm Wiz.

Continuing on FY25's strength (IB fees grew 25% Y/Y to $2.58B in 4Q25), the company foresees a robust FY2026E on AI-related consolidation and financial sponsor pent-up activity. GS has stated that it has a substantial investment banking backlog, and M&A drivers are still intact for the coming year. On the Q4 call, CEO Solomon was optimistic about FY26E as its deal pipeline coming into 2026 is at a four-year high.

While there has been uncertainty around geopolitics and interest rate paths, the volatility around 1Q26 may not dampen deal appetite, as Goldman Sachs's backlog is committed, meaning that the deals will likely happen, yet the timing may shift.

Private credit platform

Goldman Sachs has been building up its private credit platform with $142B in assets, aiming to double to $300B by 2029. The company has made several acquisitions and partnerships in the past few years to solidify its positioning.

The turmoils that have roiled private credit markets of late have spooked investors, bringing down quality franchises like GS. We believe GS is proactive in this space by allowing its hedge fund customers to short baskets of private credit exposure. This is a new structured product to capture volatility both-ways while also warehousing private credit risk.

Also, global co-head of private credit, James Reynolds, mentioned that asset-backed lending and investment grade private credit are the fastest growing sub-segments in 2026, and that mezzanine demand is picking up specifically because the overall M&A market has been robust.

Marquee listings in 2026

There are several key listings potentially hitting in 2026, which include the highly anticipated IPOs for SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic. GS will no doubt be among the banks competing for lead-left roles.

In Europe, GS' EMEA division told reporters that the company has many potential IPO deals with European companies. So, the IPO boom is not just in the U.S. but also around other major equity markets.

CEO David Solomon anticipates ECM levels in FY2026E to exceed that of FY2025, but will remain below the 2021 peak.

How exposed is GS AM to redemptions?

Unlike Ares (ARES), Blue Owl (OWL) and Blackstone (BX), GS is much better positioned and well managed. GS has a material $22B implied software credit book, according to a February 27 letter sent to its AM clients. But the redemption rate is lower than peers, and inflows remain positive.

Metric Goldman Sachs Peer Average
Software/tech credit exposure 15.5% of portfolio (Q3 2025) High end of peer range
Q4 2025 redemption rate 3.5% 5%+ at competitors
December 2025 inflows +11% above YTD average Declining at most peers
Fund structure Primarily closed-end institutional Peers more retail semi-liquid

Sources: Bloomberg, market sources, Himalayas Research estimates

Generally speaking, Goldman Sachs targets companies with $100M+ EBITDA, whose default rates are meaningfully lower than the smaller firms. In terms of fund structure and clientele, Goldman's funds are primarily closed-end and offered to institutional clients, avoiding the redemption spiral risk hitting peers' semi-liquid retail BDCs.

Valuation and verdict

On a forward P/E basis, GS is attractively valued against MS and JPM, despite having similar or arguably superior margins for their markets business. GS is also cheaper valued on P/TBV, mainly due to its still-maturing asset/wealth management business, whose segment margins are behind the leader—Morgan Stanley's 30%.

We believe GS has the most favorable prime brokerage and equity financing franchise, which will likely do well in volatile, moderate downturn environments. Its banking division has a strong pipeline coming into 2026, while its AWM is ramping up and provides market upside exposure, ready to capture a larger share of the market AUM with its growing private credit and alternatives platform.

We rate GS a buy.

data.title: Nomura Core Equity Fund: Q4 2025 Buys, Sells, And Standouts
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Nomura Core Equity Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

For 4Q25, Nomura Core Equity Fund Institutional Class shares underperformed the Fund's benchmark, the S&P 500 Index. Stock selection drove 90% of the underperformance. Selections were weakest in consumer discretionary, financials, and communication services. Sector allocation decisions were modestly negative with our underweight of healthcare, which outperformed, and overweight of financials, which underperformed, affecting performance. Cash was a small drag on performance given the modest quarterly gain in the market.

Leading contributors on a relative basis to the S&P 500 follow:

Applied Materials Inc. (AMAT) shares increased on positive revisions to 2026 and 2027 semiconductor capital spending plans, a response to surging demand for AI-related chips.

Cummins Inc (CMI). shares experienced steady price gains and positive momentum due to strong revenue and earnings growth, particularly in its power-generation business, which has significant datacenter exposure.

Oracle Corp. (ORCL) shares declined during the quarter. Our lack of ownership was beneficial as investors grew concerned about customer concentration with OpenAI and the large external financing required to fund an aggressive AI infrastructure buildout.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) Co. Ltd. shares continued to appreciate on broad leadership in leading-edge semiconductor manufacturing and positive exposure to key themes within technology including AI buildout.

Advanced Micro Devices Inc (AMD). shares advanced during the quarter on a large customer commitment to the company's next generation AI accelerator chips and indications that customers want competitive options to Nvidia (NVDA) chips.

Leading detractors on a relative basis to the S&P 500 follow:

Fiserv Inc (FI) (FI). shares declined on a material downward revision of 2025 and 2026 growth expectations and the departure of the CFO and three board members. The company admitted to unnaturally boosting prior year growth in the Clover merchant acceptance businesses as well as aggressive growth tactics in the financial technology segment. While the new management team appears to have come clean on suspect accounting and disclosure practices, we exited our position based on low conviction in our prior growth estimates.

AutoZone Inc (AZO). shares declined during the quarter on accelerated investment in future growth via the buildout of new stores and "hubs" that will serve the company's commercial market-share ambitions. These investments are negatively affecting near-term earnings.

Eli Lilly and Co. (LLY) shares surged on a price agreement with the government for its key weight loss drugs. The deal appears to have de-risked near-term growth. Given our long-term concern about rising competition and decelerating growth, we did not own shares in the company and thus did not benefit.

BAE Systems PLC (BAESY) shares declined on speculation that Ukraine and Russia were coming closer to an agreement to end the war. Our thesis in owning BAE is much longer-term in nature and is reliant on a generation shift in defense spending intentions in Europe.

Eaton Corp (ETN). PLC shares declined on disclosure that investments in new manufacturing capacity and acquisitions will limit near-term upward earnings revisions though the company continues to see a robust business environment tied to data-center construction, for which the company provides electrical products.

Positions Added:

Accenture PLC (ACN) is a global technology consulting and managed services business that has thrived during previous periods of technological disruption and change. While early, we believe the need for businesses everywhere to adapt and invest in AI capabilities will enable accelerated growth for Accenture.

Entergy Corp. (ETR) is an electric utility focused on the Southern region of the US including portions of Arkansas, Louisiana, Mississippi, and Texas. We like Entergy's accelerating earnings growth tied to regulated, low-risk capacity investment and contracted backlog of new gas generation capacity to serve large data-center customers. This historic mid-single digit earnings grower should accelerate to the low-double-digits over the coming years.

Ferguson Enterprises Inc. (FERG) is a high-quality distributor of plumbing, infrastructure, HVAC, and appliances. We are optimistic about future growth and valuation upside due to exposure to non-residential investment, including large infrastructure projects, and a bottoming in residential activity. Future inclusion in the S&P500 is an upside technical catalyst.

Gilead Sciences Inc. (GILD) was purchased based on strong expertise in HIV treatment and a revolutionary HIV prevention product, Yeztugo, that we believe will drive meaningful upside earnings revisions.

Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (TMO) is likely to benefit from improving market conditions for life sciences tools following a three-year post Covid "hangover."

Position Eliminated:

Allstate Corp. (ALL)

ConocoPhillips (COP)

Fiserv Inc.

Linde PLC (LIN)

The Sherwin-Williams Co. (SHW)

Zebra Technologies Corp. (ZBRA)

The prices of growth stocks may rise or fall dramatically and may be more volatile, particularly over the short term. A "value stock" is a stock that is believed to be undervalued. Investments in value stocks are subject to the risk that the stocks are appropriately valued or that the stocks may decline in value. The value of securities in a particular industry or sector will decline because of changing expectations for the performance of that industry or sector. Issuers in the information technology sector may be impacted by intense competition; dramatic and often unpredictable changes in growth rates and competition for qualified personnel; effects on profitability from being heavily dependent on patent and intellectual property rights and the loss or impairment of those rights; obsolescence of existing technology; general economic conditions; and government regulation. Foreign securities (particularly in emerging markets) may be adversely affected by political instability, changes in currency exchange rates, inefficient markets and higher transaction costs, foreign economic conditions, the imposition of economic or trade sanctions, or inadequate or different regulatory and accounting standards. A fund or strategy that holds a limited number of securities may be more sensitive to the price fluctuations of a single security in its portfolio and may be more volatile than a fund or strategy that holds a greater number of securities. The fund or strategy is subject to liquidity risk, which is the risk that the fund or strategy's investments cannot be readily sold within seven calendar days at approximately the price at which the fund or strategy has valued them.

data.title: Kanzhun: A 'Buy' On Profitability Surprise And Attractive Yield
data.description:
I've stayed bullish on Kanzhun Limited (BZ) (2076.HK).

Its operating profitability surprised on the upside in the recent quarter. The stock's FY26 capital management (buybacks/dividends) yield is an appealing mid-single digit.

My November 20, 2025 update highlighted that this Chinese "recruitment platform" operator delivered above-expectations 3Q25 results.

I'm Impressed With Quarterly Margin Enhancement

BZ filed a 6-K on March 18 unveiling its latest numbers.

The group's normalized "Operating Profit/OP" was 37% higher year-on-year to CNY 0.90B during the Oct-Dec '25 period. This represents a 7% beat against S&P Capital IQ consensus.

Also, its 4Q2025 OP-to-sales widened by 7.2ppts to 43.3% on a YoY basis. That's 2.5ppts ahead of the sell-side's 40.8% projection.

In my view, growth acceleration and leaner operations were responsible for its solid performance.

BZ's recent three-month topline of CNY 2,079M came in above the upper-end of the CNY 2.05-2.07B management guide. The implied 14.0% YoY rise was faster than the +13.2% seen for the preceding quarter. I believe that efforts to deepen its presence in specific client and geographical segments are paying off.

The firm shared at the analyst meeting that 4Q25 "revenue contribution from third-tier and low-tier cities approached 25%." The same metric was at a low-teens level in '21.

Sales generated by "enterprises with fewer than 100 employees" were up 21% YoY in Q4 according to its briefing disclosures. That's better than the 14% company-wide increase.

Separately, its total OpEx declined 7% YoY to CNY 1.36B for the final quarter of the previous year.

The "Sales & Marketing" or "S&M" costs-to-revenue for BZ went down from 4Q2024's 20% to 4Q2025's 16%. At the results call, it explained that strengthened "sales efficiency" and the absence of "major marketing campaigns" contributed to this substantial reduction.

Positive Indicators For The Year Ahead

Analysts are currently forecasting that BZ's turnover expansion will improve from +12% for FY25 to +13% in FY26. I think's that's achievable. These figures were obtained from S&P Capital IQ.

UBS Group (UBS) issued a note (inaccessible in public) titled "China Internet Sector: Online Recruitment" on March 11, 2026. The European bank polled over 200 Mainland Chinese corporate officers towards the end of last year. It found that the proportion of respondents "planning to increase spending" on Kanzhun's portal increased from 3Q25's 43% to 4Q25's 54%. This metric for its other rivals fell during the same timeframe.

The amount of time users devoted to BZ's mobile application jumped 60% YoY in the first month of '26. The December 2025 growth rate was +18%. I sourced these statistics from Nomura's (NMR) Feb 24 report (not publicly available) named "Monthly App Tracker." NMR's research also indicated that competing domestic platforms like "51Job" and "Zhaopin" witnessed relatively weaker mid-teens increases in time spent for January.

You should be aware of management's favorable market observations too. BZ mentioned at the Q4 briefing that its site's "average daily job postings in the 15 days after the spring festival grew (YoY) by a double-digit percentage."

I'm encouraged by its successful venture across the border. Kanzhun is already an established leader in Mainland China as outlined above. Its earnings call remarks suggested that the sub-brand "OfferToday has now ranked #1 in Hong Kong in terms of daily active users." My opinion is that this could provide further upside to its 2026 revenues.

BZ Is Becoming More Investor-Friendly

On the same day of its results announcement, BZ published a press release disclosing a formal "shareholder return target." It means that "no less than 50% of the Company’s adjusted net income" will be paid out to stockholders for '26-28.

I estimate that the stock's forward shareholder yield is a reasonably decent 4.7%.

The sell-side has projected that BZ's net income will go up 10% to CNY 4.0B (source: S&P Capital IQ). I assume that the firm should distribute half of that as buybacks and dividends in the near future. The consensus prediction appears realistic to me, as I've identified signs implying that Kanzhun performed well in early-2026.

Its actual stockholder return might be even more significant. At the results briefing, BZ stressed that "we will scale up repurchase as appropriate in response to changing market conditions." I infer from this statement that it's willing to buy more shares if the stock price come under pressure due to a broad sell-down.

You Need To Watch These Downside Factors

Demand for job matching in China services could drop, if the Middle East's ongoing geopolitical crisis eventually leads to a global economic downturn.

BZ's capital allocation priorities might change over time. A potential move to set aside less cash for repurchases/dividends would disappoint yield-hunting investors.

The Final Word

I continue to rate this name as 'Buy'.

My late-Nov '25 piece benchmarked BZ's valuations with its Asian peer "Recruit Holdings" (RCRRF) (6098.T). I do the same here.

As per S&P Capital IQ, Kanzhun and RCRRF trade at next twelve months' P/Es of 11x and 17x, respectively.

BZ's potential upside is +55% (17/11) using its listed comparable as a reference. My take is that its shares deserve a re-rating considering the enhanced shareholder-friendliness and a 4Q25 OP beat.

data.title: Micron Is Rewriting The Memory Cycle
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I believe Micron (MU) is no longer a cyclical memory stock but rather a fundamentally mispriced AI infrastructure stock. I’ve been bullish on this memory cycle from early on, and since my last coverage, Micron has moved another 12% higher, reinforcing that conviction.

I see pricing, not volume, driving the growth, and this is being underpinned by ongoing supply chain issues and increasing memory intensity in AI systems. With gross margins accelerating towards 80% and long-duration customer contracts emerging, I believe there’s underlying durability in the stock that the market is currently discounting. So, if you believe this continues, Micron’s underlying earnings potential is much, much greater than what the current stock price suggests.

Pricing Power Is the Real Story, Not Volume

MU’s story has changed, and it is not about volume but about pricing, and I think this changes everything. If you really dig into the numbers, you’ll see that DRAM bit volumes were only up mid-single digits and NAND bit volumes were only up low single digits. So, not exactly a rocketship. But pricing? That’s a different story. DRAM pricing was up in the mid-60% range, and NAND pricing was up in the high 70% range. That’s where the revenue growth, and much more importantly, the margin growth, is happening.

And I think this matters because, fundamentally, pricing tends to behave differently than volume. Volume can disappear rapidly. Pricing, especially in the face of underlying supply chain issues, tends to persist for longer than people expect. Management has confirmed this as well. They are currently only able to satisfy 50% to 2/3 of the demand in some cases. This is not a balanced market but a supply-constrained market, and when you add in the gross margins that are moving up towards 80%, you can see that this is a supply issue, not a demand issue.

The Quiet Shift: Memory Is Becoming Contracted Infrastructure

I think one of the things that investors are not focusing on enough in this entire discussion is the introduction of the Strategic Customer Agreements, or SCAs. While this may not have gotten as much attention as the numbers, in retrospect, this might be just as important. Micron has just signed their first 5-year SCA, moving beyond the traditional 1-year LTAs. While this might not be a huge deal in and of itself, when you think about it, you realize that this is a big deal because the relationship between the supplier and the customer is changing.

What this is saying is that the customers, the hyperscalers, the AI infrastructure providers, cannot afford uncertainty anymore. And so they need these agreements in place so they know they have the memory they need as they are rolling out their AI infrastructure. The interesting part is that Micron is in a position where they have the power in these negotiations due to the supply constraints.

What is even more interesting is the management’s hinting at a closer working arrangement in terms of R&D and product roadmaps. I think this is where the business model begins to change. Not dramatically, perhaps, but gradually. Memory is still a hardware play, and it’s still a commodity and a critical part of AI infrastructure. However, it’s starting to feel just a little bit less like a commodity and a little bit more contracted around AI infrastructure. If that trend continues, well, that’s going to influence volatility and valuation in interesting ways.

HBM4 and the Bigger Shift: Memory Is Now the Bottleneck

Pricing may explain what’s happening in the present, but I think architecture explains what’s going to happen in the future. And what’s happening in AI architecture right now is that memory is front and center. Micron’s announcement around HBM4 which delivers over 2.8 TB/s bandwidth, is significant in and of itself.

As AI models get bigger, context windows get bigger, and inference workloads get bigger, suddenly we’re in a world where we need more and more memory to store and deliver that data. And that’s why the new architecture emerges that’s more memory-intensive, not in spite of memory but because of memory.

Look at AI servers; they now require significantly more memory per unit. Workstations are shipped with 128 GB memory configurations, and smartphones are rapidly migrating to 12 GB+ memory configurations, from under 20% penetration just last year. These are structural changes in memory content, not incremental changes, and what’s more, alternative architectures are not reducing memory requirements; they’re increasing memory requirements. Efficiency plays are just as memory-intensive as everything else because they require large memory pools to work at all.

So, when management talks about memory being a “defining strategic asset,” I think they’re right. That’s because, if memory is the bottleneck, then the companies controlling the bottleneck have pricing power, and this is what’s happening.

Valuation Disconnect: Pricing a Cycle, Missing a Structural Shift

I still think the stock is a strong buy because the valuation does not take into account the extent of the earnings reset currently underway. At $444/share, are only trading at 7.9x FY2026 EPS estimates and 4.8x FY2027 EPS estimates based on consensus estimates of $58 and $95.53 respectively. That is an exceptionally low valuation for a company growing revenue by 192% and 45.0% respectively in the next two years to $109.18 billion in FY2026 and $158.3 billion in FY2027.

What I find interesting is the fact that even after the strong move up, the shares are only trading at 12.6x forward P/E, around 42% lower than the sector median. Similarly, the shares are only trading at 13.41x forward GAAP P/E, 53.8% lower than the sector median. While I agree that the company is trading at an elevated price based on the EV/Sales ratio, I think the earnings are going to grow quickly, and the market is still treating the company like a memory company when the evidence is building for a much stronger story from the company's AI infrastructure segment.

The Real Risk: Supply Catches Up Faster Than Expected

I think the biggest risk is mean reversion, which means that supply grows in a faster-than-expected fashion, and/or AI demand grows in a slower-than-expected fashion. The HBM competitive landscape and how Micron competes in the higher-end segments are a risk, as is the risk of higher CapEx spending, which is now above $25 billion. There is a geopolitical risk related to export controls and the impact they have on the China demand dynamics, and frankly, the risk that the SCAs are not successful in maintaining discipline in the downturn and the market is justified in its skepticism.

However, I still don’t see supply normalizing as fast as the market is anticipating. While Micron is guiding more than $25 billion in CapEx spending, the problem isn’t the spending; it’s the time and the complexity. New fabs have long lead times, new nodes have reduced bits per wafer, and HBM has significantly higher capacity requirements per unit of output. Meanwhile, inventory levels are still tight and customers are still under-allocated, which just further confirms that supply hasn’t caught up yet.

Takeaway

I would not focus as much on the peak in the memory cycle and more on whether this is a structurally different cycle. If supply is still constrained and AI demand is still compounding, then Micron’s margins are not going to revert as fast as people might think they are. The opportunity is in understanding this before the market fully prices it in.

data.title: Rewriting The Hedge Fund Playbook: Lessons From 2008
data.description:
As the hedge fund industry hovers near an all-time high of roughly $5 trillion in assets, up from about $600 billion at the turn of the century, it has recently experienced a moderate drawdown across most strategies in the first half of March 2026. Many investors view this as a temporary setback driven by volatility linked to the Iran war. However, if this is not a short-lived disruption, and losses deepen into sustained double-digit declines, will the implications for the industry resemble those experienced during the 2008 financial crisis? During that period, widespread drawdowns triggered investor redemptions, which in turn amplified losses in less-liquid strategies and created a negative feedback loop between performance and capital flows.

The industry has evolved meaningfully since 2008, leaving it better positioned to navigate periods of market stress. The following structural changes have made a similar unravelling less likely:

Performance: Expectations vs. Reality

Prior to 2009, many hedge funds and fund-of-funds marketed their strategies as delivering absolute returns, often targeting benchmarks such as T-bills plus 400 basis points. This framing led investors to expect consistent positive performance regardless of market conditions. As a result, many were surprised when the average hedge fund declined approximately 18% in 2008, triggering significant withdrawals. Redemption risk increases when realized outcomes diverge sharply from expectations.

Since then, managers have set more realistic expectations and educated investors on how their strategies are likely to perform across different market environments. Many investors now understand that the majority of hedge fund strategies exhibit some degree of correlation to broader markets, especially during market stress, and are therefore more prepared to withstand periods of underperformance.

Due Diligence and Transparency

In the early 2000s, operational due diligence was often limited and, in some cases, largely overlooked. Investment decisions were frequently made after only one or two meetings, with a primary emphasis on performance rather than operational robustness. Transparency was also constrained, with many managers providing limited visibility into their investment processes or underlying positions, resulting in an industry heavily reliant on trust.

This approach changed dramatically following two pivotal events: the Bernie Madoff Ponzi scheme and the Lehman Brothers collapse. Together, these events exposed significant weaknesses in both due diligence and operational infrastructure. In response, investors have adopted far more rigorous and institutionalized processes, placing greater emphasis on transparency, independent verification, and risk management. As a result, manager selection has become more disciplined, and overall industry integrity has improved.

Due Diligence Reforms After the Madoff Fraud

The uncovering of the Bernie Madoff Ponzi scheme sent shockwaves throughout the hedge fund industry, triggering significant redemptions, particularly from investors in fund-of-funds with direct or indirect exposure. The episode exposed fundamental shortcomings in due diligence practices, especially among intermediaries whose primary role was manager selection and oversight.

Beyond the immediate losses, the fraud undermined broader investor confidence. Many allocators recognized that they had not conducted sufficiently rigorous due diligence and, in some cases, lacked a clear understanding of underlying exposures due to limited transparency. This uncertainty raised concerns about undiscovered risks elsewhere in the industry, prompting further redemptions and a reduction in allocations.

Prime Brokerage Custody Structures and Counterparty Risk

In the aftermath of the Lehman Brothers collapse, many hedge funds were forced to confront the complexities of their prime brokerage custody arrangements. In some instances, assets were held in the name of the prime broker as rehypothecated collateral, enabling brokers to facilitate financing, support leverage, and enable short-selling.

While effective in normal conditions, this structure introduced significant counterparty risk. When Lehman failed, certain assets were treated as part of the firm’s general estate, requiring hedge funds to stand alongside other unsecured creditors in recovery proceedings. This highlighted the importance of asset segregation and legal ownership. In response, investors have strengthened operational due diligence, with a focus on custody structures, rehypothecation terms, and the quality of service providers, improving overall investor protection.

Alignment of Liquidity Terms and Underlying Investments

In the years leading up to the 2008 financial crisis, many hedge funds offered monthly liquidity with 30-day notice periods to attract fund-of-funds capital. In doing so, managers often overlooked the mismatch between offered liquidity and the liquidity of underlying investments.

This misalignment remained manageable during periods of positive inflows but was exposed when allocations halted and redemptions surged. Many funds were forced to impose gates or suspend redemptions, which eroded investor confidence. Funds that honored liquidity terms were disproportionately burdened by withdrawals and became sources of liquidity for investors. In response, both managers and allocators now focus on aligning liquidity terms with underlying assets, with investors more willing to accept longer lock-ups for less-liquid strategies and less tolerant of structural mismatches.

Reduced Use of Leverage by Investors and Managers

Heading into 2008, many hedge fund investors (especially fund-of-funds) used leverage to enhance returns, assuming diversified portfolios would deliver stable performance. This approach proved highly vulnerable during market stress.

As losses mounted, leverage amplified declines and triggered margin calls, forcing investors to redeem capital from underlying managers. These pressures were compounded by concurrent redemptions from their own clients, creating a cascading effect. Many fund-of-funds were forced to deleverage, experienced significant outflows, or exited the market. Since then, leverage at the investor level has declined, with a greater emphasis on liquidity, transparency, and risk management.

Evolution in the Composition and Quality of the Investor Base

At the time of the 2008 financial crisis, the hedge fund investor base was dominated by fund-of-funds, high-net worth individuals, and family offices, which are groups often viewed as more reactive to short-term performance. This contributed to rapid outflows during periods of stress.

The investor base has since shifted toward more stable institutional capital. Pension funds, along with endowments, foundations, sovereign wealth funds, and OCIO platforms, now represent the majority of industry assets. These investors typically have longer time horizons and more structured decision-making processes, contributing to greater stability.

Additionally, the quality of capital has improved. Endowments and foundations have strengthened liquidity management following challenges in 2008 related to private equity allocations and capital call dynamics. Fund-of-funds have also undergone significant consolidation, with remaining players adopting more rigorous due diligence, reduced leverage, and improved investor communication. Collectively, these changes have resulted in a more resilient and better-informed investor base.

Increased Concentration and Crowding Risk

While the industry is more operationally robust than it was in the early 2000s, there has been an increasing flow of capital to a small number of the largest multi-manager platforms. Larger investors appreciate the scale of these pod shops which can manage larger ticket sizes. Additionally, by hedging out risk across their portfolio in real time, multi-manager firms can mitigate drawdowns during moderate periods of stress.

However, many of these firms hold overlapping positions in the same securities, and during a major market event, selling by one firm can generate losses for others following similar strategies. In this environment, simultaneous de-risking across the multi-manager platforms can lead to unexpectedly large losses. This risk will be put to the test during the next major market sell-off, and allocators can mitigate their exposure by allocating to less-crowded strategies and differentiated managers.

Summary

Hedge fund industry net flows will prove more resilient in a sustained drawdown than they were during the 2008 financial crisis. Rather than broad industry outflows, we would see a rotation of capital between managers as investors reposition portfolios toward strategies perceived to be more resilient while redeeming from underperformers. In this environment, industry assets may remain stable overall, but the benefits will accrue disproportionately to high-quality managers.
